They are rare, iconic, and immediately recognizable, representing the artist at the height of his mature style. The 1917–1918 nude series is central to his oeuvre and to modern art more broadly: elongated anatomy, sculptural simplification, a highly controlled contour line, sensual but monumental presentation of the female body, and the balance between archaic idealization and modern erotic directness. These are the pictures that define Modigliani for the broad market and for major museums.\n\nFrom the image provided, the composition strongly aligns with the celebrated reclining nude type associated with his late Paris period. The warm red ground, abbreviated setting, black hair, mask-like face, almond eyes, and flattened but highly sensual treatment of flesh all correspond to recognized characteristics of Modigliani’s practice. The work’s artistic quality, judged only from the image, appears high: the figure fills the picture plane confidently, the contours are elegant and assured, and the chromatic contrast between flesh tones and the deep terracotta-red background creates a powerful visual identity. If this image corresponds to the known canonical work often referred to as the “Red Nude,” then the painting would sit among the artist’s most sought-after pictures.\n\nCondition cannot be properly established from this low-resolution image, but there are visible surface abrasions, possible paint loss, and areas of wear or restoration, particularly near the lower edge and darker passages. However, for a masterpiece of this caliber, condition issues, unless extreme, tend to have a comparatively limited effect on value relative to authenticity, title, date, scale, and provenance. Still, in the absence of a formal condition report, one must reserve against overvaluation. Modigliani’s Nu couche (1917–1918) sold at Christie’s New York in 2015 for approximately $170.4 million including fees, becoming one of the highest auction prices ever achieved for a work of art. Another major reclining nude, Nu couche (sur le cote gauche), sold at Sotheby’s in 2018 for roughly $157.2 million. These results establish a benchmark for premier Modigliani nudes. Lesser portraits and non-iconic works by Modigliani sell for substantially less, but the reclining nudes are a category unto themselves. Since then, despite fluctuations in the trophy-art market, ultra-blue-chip masterpieces by universally recognized artists have remained highly competitive, particularly when they are fresh to market and museum-quality.\n\nWithout provenance, literature references, or exhibition history, prudence is essential. Those factors can move value dramatically: a documented chain of ownership, inclusion in the Ceroni catalogue raisonne, major exhibition history, and long-term private collection provenance would support the top of the range; unresolved title issues, gaps around the wartime period, or questions of attribution would collapse the valuation entirely. Because the prompt asks for fair market value assuming authenticity, I am valuing this as a major, fully accepted Modigliani reclining nude of the 1917–1918 group. parsed.internal_reasoning:
Assuming the work is authentic, legally transferable, and correctly identified as Amedeo Modigliani’s Nu couche from 1917–1918, this would be one of the most valuable paintings ever to come to market. Modigliani’s reclining nudes occupy the absolute top tier of 20th-century painting, both art-historically and commercially. They are rare, iconic, and immediately recognizable, representing the artist at the height of his mature style. The 1917–1918 nude series is central to his oeuvre and to modern art more broadly: elongated anatomy, sculptural simplification, a highly controlled contour line, sensual but monumental presentation of the female body, and the balance between archaic idealization and modern erotic directness. These are the pictures that define Modigliani for the broad market and for major museums.

From the image provided, the composition strongly aligns with the celebrated reclining nude type associated with his late Paris period. The warm red ground, abbreviated setting, black hair, mask-like face, almond eyes, and flattened but highly sensual treatment of flesh all correspond to recognized characteristics of Modigliani’s practice. The work’s artistic quality, judged only from the image, appears high: the figure fills the picture plane confidently, the contours are elegant and assured, and the chromatic contrast between flesh tones and the deep terracotta-red background creates a powerful visual identity. If this image corresponds to the known canonical work often referred to as the “Red Nude,” then the painting would sit among the artist’s most sought-after pictures.

Condition cannot be properly established from this low-resolution image, but there are visible surface abrasions, possible paint loss, and areas of wear or restoration, particularly near the lower edge and darker passages. However, for a masterpiece of this caliber, condition issues, unless extreme, tend to have a comparatively limited effect on value relative to authenticity, title, date, scale, and provenance. Still, in the absence of a formal condition report, one must reserve against overvaluation. A major Modigliani nude would be expected to have conservation history; any relining, retouching, thinning of paint, or damage would materially affect bidding, but not necessarily remove it from the nine-figure category if the work is a prime example.

The market comparables are extraordinarily strong. Modigliani’s Nu couche (1917–1918) sold at Christie’s New York in 2015 for approximately $170.4 million including fees, becoming one of the highest auction prices ever achieved for a work of art. Another major reclining nude, Nu couche (sur le cote gauche), sold at Sotheby’s in 2018 for roughly $157.2 million. These results establish a benchmark for premier Modigliani nudes. Lesser portraits and non-iconic works by Modigliani sell for substantially less, but the reclining nudes are a category unto themselves. Since then, despite fluctuations in the trophy-art market, ultra-blue-chip masterpieces by universally recognized artists have remained highly competitive, particularly when they are fresh to market and museum-quality.

Without provenance, literature references, or exhibition history, prudence is essential. Those factors can move value dramatically: a documented chain of ownership, inclusion in the Ceroni catalogue raisonne, major exhibition history, and long-term private collection provenance would support the top of the range; unresolved title issues, gaps around the wartime period, or questions of attribution would collapse the valuation entirely. Because the prompt asks for fair market value assuming authenticity, I am valuing this as a major, fully accepted Modigliani reclining nude of the 1917–1918 group. On that basis, a realistic hammer-plus-premium outcome at a major international auction today would likely fall in the broad $140 million to $190 million range depending on condition, guarantees, competitive tension, and macro-market sentiment. My single-point estimate is set near the middle-upper part of that range because of the image’s apparent quality and the enduring desirability of this specific subject within Modigliani’s market.
key_factors: Iconic 1917–1918 reclining nude series central to Modigliani’s market and legacy
Strong auction comparables for top-tier Modigliani nudes in the $150M+ range
High visual quality and classic stylistic hallmarks visible in the image
Potential condition concerns visible, but likely secondary to rarity and significance
Value heavily contingent on full authentication, catalogue inclusion, and clean provenance
